worth_it_explanation: Supply source configuration in The Trade Desk directly shapes which impressions receive bids, what the account pays for them, and whether the account bids against itself through redundant paths. Enabling every available exchange by default routes spend into duplicative and lower-quality paths without deliberate review. OpenPath integrations shorten the chain to direct publisher connections where publishers support it, which improves both the quality signal and the economics. These checks take a single settings review in the platform and a look at active supply source rows to complete.
intro: The Trade Desk routes programmatic spend through supply sources selected in each ad group: SSP integrations, private marketplace (PMP) deals, and OpenPath direct publisher connections. OpenPath is The Trade Desk's initiative to connect buyers directly to publisher ad servers, shortening the supply path and reducing the number of intermediary fees between advertiser and impression. These checks confirm that the supply source selection is intentional rather than every-exchange-enabled by default, that OpenPath is active for publishers who support it, and that duplicate supply paths to the same impression are consolidated so the account does not compete against itself in the same auction.

audit_checks.title: Supply sources are not deliberately selected, and all or most exchanges are active by default
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When every available exchange is active on an ad group, spend flows to duplicative and low-quality supply paths alongside quality ones. The account cannot easily distinguish where its budget performed and loses the ability to concentrate spend on supply sources with verified quality and pricing transparency.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk allows buyers to select which supply sources receive bids at the ad group level. The options include individual SSP integrations, OpenPath (direct publisher connections), and private marketplace (PMP) deals. Leaving all or most exchanges active without a deliberate selection means the account bids across supply paths that vary significantly in quality, fee structure, and auction transparency.

Enabling every exchange may appear to maximize reach, but it routes spend to paths with weaker quality signals alongside paths with strong ones. Without deliberate selection, the reporting does not cleanly separate which supply relationships delivered the outcomes the campaign needed, and the account has limited visibility into how supply-path costs affected the effective cost per result.

A deliberate supply source strategy typically includes a small set of SSPs the account has evaluated for quality and pricing, any PMP deals negotiated for specific inventory, and OpenPath integrations where publishers support the direct connection. This does not require restricting to a single source. It requires that the selection reflects a considered choice rather than the platform default.

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • The ad group's supply source settings show all exchanges enabled with no manual curation of the list
  • The campaign has no PMP deals and no OpenPath integration active, relying entirely on open-market SSP supply
  • The supply source report shows spend distributed widely across many SSPs with no concentration in evaluated sources
You may want to review which supply sources are active and whether that selection was intentional, then remove sources that were not deliberately added.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce advertisers, supply source discipline matters at the bottom of the funnel. Retargeting and prospecting campaigns that require high-quality placements benefit from a tighter supply list. Unfiltered exchange coverage introduces placements that do not carry the same quality bar. You may want to confirm that the ad groups running conversion-focused campaigns have a curated source list rather than open-exchange defaults.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead generation campaigns, brand context affects conversion intent. A supply source list that concentrates spend on evaluated, brand-safe publishers supports conversion quality. You may want to confirm that ad groups running lead-gen campaigns are not bidding on the full exchange list without review.

learn_more.source_title: Sellers.json and the OpenRTB SupplyChain object

investigate.intro: Review each ad group's supply source settings to determine whether the active exchanges reflect a deliberate selection or the platform default.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign and navigate to an ad group. Select 'Inventory' or 'Supply Sources' in the ad group settings.
Review the list of active supply sources. Note whether all or most available SSPs are enabled, or whether the selection reflects a deliberate set of evaluated partners and deals.
If OpenPath is listed as an option for any relevant publishers, note whether it is active.
Check the Reporting tab filtered by supply vendor. Note whether spend is broadly distributed across many SSPs or concentrated in a smaller, curated set.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group reviewed, whether the supply source selection was deliberate (a curated list including OpenPath where available, PMP deals, and evaluated SSPs) or the platform default (all or most exchanges enabled without manual selection). An ad group with a curated supply list passes. An ad group with all exchanges enabled and no evidence of deliberate selection is a fail, recorded with the ad group name and the count of active sources.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the supply source configuration for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that supply source selection on {{site_name}}'s ad groups defaults to all or most available exchanges rather than a deliberate, curated set. Bidding across every exchange without review routes spend to supply paths that vary in quality and fee structure alongside evaluated ones, which limits the account's ability to concentrate spend where quality and pricing have been assessed. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the active supply sources on each ad group, removing exchanges that were not deliberately added, activating OpenPath integrations for publishers who support the direct connection, and confirming that any PMP deals in use are reflected in the source list.

audit_checks.title: OpenPath is not active on ad groups where supported publishers are available
audit_checks.impact_rationale: OpenPath connects buyers directly to publisher ad servers, bypassing SSP intermediary fees and improving the account's visibility into the supply chain. Not activating it where publishers support the integration means the account pays more intermediary costs and forfeits the pricing transparency the direct connection provides.

educate.base:
OpenPath is The Trade Desk's supply-path program that integrates directly with participating publishers' ad servers. When an impression comes through OpenPath rather than through an SSP, the supply path is shorter: the publisher connects directly to The Trade Desk without an SSP taking a share of the impression value in between. The buyer sees the same auction but with fewer intermediary fees deducted from the publisher's revenue and a cleaner view of where the impression originated.

The practical benefit for the buyer is a more transparent supply chain and, for publishers who have joined the program, a stronger quality signal for the inventory. Because the publisher connects directly, the ad server confirmation that an impression came from a specific publisher domain is more reliable than it would be through an SSP that handles traffic from many sources simultaneously.

OpenPath does not replace all supply. Not every publisher has joined the program, and open-market SSP supply continues to provide reach that OpenPath alone cannot match. The relevant question in an audit is whether OpenPath is active for the publishers who do support it, and whether that direct channel is included alongside rather than instead of SSP coverage.

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • The ad group's supply source list does not include an OpenPath entry, or OpenPath is listed but set to inactive
  • The account's supply source report shows no impressions attributed to OpenPath integrations
  • OpenPath-eligible publishers are running as open-market supply through an SSP when a direct connection would be available
You may want to confirm whether OpenPath is active on ad groups where the direct connection could apply, then add it as an active source if it is not already included.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce campaigns, OpenPath integrations with premium publishers can improve the signal quality for prospecting and retargeting. You may want to confirm that OpenPath is active on ad groups targeting those publishers so the direct connection is used where it is available.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead generation campaigns that prioritize publisher context, OpenPath integrations with relevant publishers provide a more direct view of where impressions originate. You may want to confirm that OpenPath is active on ad groups where publisher context matters to lead quality.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether OpenPath is listed as an active supply source on the ad groups being audited and whether impressions have come through the direct connection.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the ad group and navigate to its supply source settings. Look for OpenPath in the list of available sources and note whether it is active.

In the Reporting tab, filter by supply vendor and look for impressions attributed to OpenPath. If OpenPath is listed but the impression count is zero or absent, the integration may be active in settings but not receiving bids in practice.

If OpenPath is not in the supply source list at all, note this as the finding. OpenPath availability depends on which publishers have joined the program, so its absence may reflect a deliberate decision or an oversight.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, whether OpenPath is listed and active in the supply source settings, and whether the supply source report shows impressions through the direct connection. An ad group with OpenPath active and impressions attributed to it passes. An ad group where OpenPath is available but not active, or active but receiving no impressions, is a fail recorded with the ad group name and the current OpenPath status.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the OpenPath status and impression count for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that OpenPath is not active on ad groups in {{site_name}}'s account where the direct publisher connection is available. OpenPath shortens the supply path to participating publishers, reducing intermediary fees and improving supply-chain transparency. Without it, impressions from supported publishers route through SSP intermediaries rather than the direct connection. {{voice_recommend}} activating OpenPath as a supply source on the affected ad groups and confirming in the supply source report that impressions begin flowing through the direct channel.

audit_checks.title: Multiple supply paths to the same publisher impression create duplicate bid exposure
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the same impression is reachable through both OpenPath and one or more SSPs simultaneously, the account may submit multiple bids into auctions for the same underlying impression. This drives up the effective cost of winning and may inflate reported impression counts if deduplication is incomplete.

educate.base:
A publisher can make the same ad impression available through multiple paths: through OpenPath directly, through one SSP, and potentially through additional SSPs that also carry that publisher's inventory. If the buyer has all of these paths active simultaneously, the demand-side platform may see the same impression appear in several bid requests, each from a different source, and bid on them independently.

The IAB Tech Lab's sellers.json and SupplyChain object standards were designed to make this visible by requiring intermediaries to declare their relationships with sellers. A buyer who reads the supply chain for an impression can see whether two bid requests for what appears to be the same publisher inventory are actually coming from the same underlying impression through different paths. The Trade Desk uses these signals, but the buyer's supply source configuration also affects how many paths are active for a given publisher.

Duplicate supply paths inflate the apparent competition for an impression and can raise the price the account pays. They also make reporting less clean. A single end impression may produce spend records across multiple supply vendors. A supply source configuration that uses OpenPath for a publisher and simultaneously leaves that publisher's inventory active through multiple SSPs is a configuration worth examining.

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • The supply source report shows spend on the same publisher domain spread across OpenPath and two or more SSPs in the same time window
  • The ad group has both OpenPath and a broad list of SSPs active, with no deliberate consolidation for publishers who joined OpenPath
  • Impression counts from a publisher appear higher than the publisher's reported traffic would support
You may want to review the supply source report filtered by publisher domain and note whether the same domain appears under both OpenPath and multiple SSP sources. Where it does, consider whether a consolidated path is preferable.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce advertisers running retargeting at scale, duplicate supply paths for the same publisher can inflate retargeting costs as the account bids redundantly for the same users. You may want to review supply source overlap on high-spend publishers in the retargeting ad groups.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead generation campaigns, duplicate paths affect CPL accuracy because the same impression may record spend across multiple supply vendors. You may want to confirm that the supply source report reflects a clean per-impression count rather than duplicated records from overlapping paths.

learn_more.source_title: Sellers.json and the OpenRTB SupplyChain object

investigate.intro: Review the supply source report for publishers where OpenPath and multiple SSPs are both active and note whether the same publisher domain appears under more than one supply path.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the Reporting tab and run a supply vendor report filtered to the ad groups under review. Add publisher domain or site as a dimension if available.
Look for publisher domains that appear under both OpenPath and one or more SSP supply vendors in the same reporting period.

Note the impression and spend split across those paths for the same publisher. A publisher with significant spend split between OpenPath and two or more SSPs in the same window may indicate active duplicate paths.
Return to the ad group's supply source settings and review whether the SSPs carrying that publisher's inventory can be narrowed or consolidated now that OpenPath is active for that publisher.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per publisher domain where OpenPath is active, whether the same domain also shows spend through multiple SSP supply paths in the same reporting window. A publisher domain whose spend routes exclusively through OpenPath, or through a single deliberate SSP source, passes. A publisher domain with spend split across OpenPath and two or more SSPs simultaneously, with no evidence of deliberate multi-path intent, is a fail recorded with the publisher domain and the paths involved.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the supply path split per publisher domain above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s account has active duplicate supply paths to the same publisher impressions, with spend on the same publisher domains split across OpenPath and multiple SSPs simultaneously. This configuration means the account may bid on the same underlying impression through more than one path, which drives up effective cost and produces fragmented supply vendor reporting for a single impression. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the supply source settings for ad groups where OpenPath is active, identifying publishers whose inventory is also coming through multiple SSPs, and consolidating to a single preferred path per publisher where possible.
